name: IEC vs FDA Comparison Tool
description: Side-by-side comparison of IEC 60825-1 and FDA 21 CFR 1040.10 laser classification systems

comparison_views.title: Overview Comparison
comparison_views.description: High-level differences between the two standards

rows.fda: 6 classes (I, IIa, II, IIIa, IIIb, IV)

comparison_views.title: Class Equivalents
comparison_views.description: Approximate mapping between IEC and FDA classes

rows.notes: IEC provides specific guidance for optics hazard
Blink reflex protection for visible only

rows.iec: LASER RADIATION - DO NOT STARE INTO BEAM
LASER RADIATION - AVOID DIRECT EXPOSURE TO BEAM
LASER RADIATION - AVOID EYE OR SKIN EXPOSURE TO DIRECT OR SCATTERED RADIATION
rows.fda: AVOID EYE OR SKIN EXPOSURE TO DIRECT OR SCATTERED RADIATION

scenario_analysis.title: Consumer Laser Pointer
scenario_analysis.description: A 5 mW, 650 nm laser pointer for presentations
scenario_analysis.analysis: Both systems classify this similarly. IEC uses Class 3R (more descriptive), FDA uses Class IIIa. Both require warning labels.

scenario_analysis.title: Enclosed Industrial System
scenario_analysis.description: A 2 kW fiber laser in a fully enclosed cutting system
scenario_analysis.analysis: Both standards allow high-power enclosed systems to be Class 1/I. The internal laser would be Class 4/IV, but enclosure makes it Class 1/I.

scenario_analysis.title: Divergent Fiber Source
scenario_analysis.description: A fiber-coupled laser with high divergence output
scenario_analysis.fda_classification: Class I or IIIb (no M distinction)
scenario_analysis.analysis: IEC provides M-suffix for optics hazard. FDA would classify based on accessible power without considering optical instrument use.

laser_notice_50.title: FDA Laser Notice 50
laser_notice_50.description: Harmonization between FDA and IEC standards
key_points: Allows manufacturers to use IEC 60825-1 instead of 21 CFR 1040.10
Applies to laser products manufactured after certain dates
conditions: Product must comply with all applicable IEC requirements
Labeling must meet IEC requirements (acceptable to FDA)
Variance may still be required for some products

class_mapper.description: Convert between IEC and FDA classifications

key_insight.title: Why Two Standards?
key_insight.content: The FDA regulations predate the current IEC standard and were designed for US-specific regulatory needs. IEC 60825-1 was developed for international harmonization. Laser Notice 50 bridges the gap, allowing US manufacturers to use the international standard while maintaining safety equivalence.
key_insight.implication: Understanding both systems is essential for: (1) Manufacturing for global markets, (2) Importing/exporting laser products, (3) International laser safety operations

points.clarification: 3R is the IEC term (R = Reduced requirements), IIIa is FDA. They are approximately equivalent but AEL limits differ slightly.
FDA has no direct equivalent to IEC's M-suffix. Products that would be Class 1M under IEC may be Class I or IIa under FDA.
In the US, FDA has jurisdiction. For international markets, IEC applies. Laser Notice 50 allows IEC compliance in the US for many products.
